title: The Brew Shop - Weekly Email Update
contentHtml:

The Brew Shop Weekly Update

Hey Brew Shop Fam!
Here's an update of what's happening this week - from new beer drops, wine reviews, our current tap list, to upcoming tastings, and more. 

SHOP THIS WEEK’S NEWSLETTER HERE
SHOP OUR ENTIRE STOCK OF BEER, WINE & HOMEBREW HERE
 
Spring Wine Fest!

TODAY! Join us for The Brew Shop's Annual Spring Wine Festival on Thursday, May 14 from 5:30 - 8:30 pm!

- Walk-about tasting featuring 20 different Spring-forward wines

- Outdoors and indoors! Enjoy the Spring weather with outdoor tasting stations along our front sidewalk

- Featuring artisanal cheese and crackers

- Discounts for retail purchases on all wines

- Chat, learn and sip wine with our wonderful team of in-the-know wine distributors

- A great way to stock up for Spring outings, family gatherings and patio time

To give us a little more space, the tasting has been split into two sessions: 5:30-7 pm and 7-8:30 pm. Tickets are $20 per person ($15 for Wine Club Members). Please choose the time slot that works best for you!

Wine Club Member? Remember to put in your special code at check out for 10% off!

PURCHASE SPRING WINE FEST TICKETS HERE
 
Friday Free Tasting!

New wines in the door!

Mary Taylor Chenonceaux Sauvignon Blanc: It's rare for a new AOC to pop up in France (you know, rules and traditions), but thanks to the work of local growers like the man behind this bottle, Luc Poullain, Touraine Chenonceaux gained appellation status in 2011. Known for it's chalky deposits, I happily and confidently declare that Chenonceaux rivals Sancerre. Linear and vibrant with passion fruit, peach and chalk-forward minerality. Sustainable.

Maison Fabre Cotes de Provence Rose: I'm pretty certain two out of the three of us let out an audible swoon when we saw the blue accents on this classic Provencal Rose. Produced by a family with a remarkable 17 generations of winemaking history, Maison Fabre draws from three distinct Provençal terroirs — calcareous hills inland, breezy coastal vineyards, and sandstone slopes swept by the Mistral — for a wine that is dry, bright and racy with fresh red berries. Sustainable.

Populis Wabi Sabi: Shaunt and Diego are 100% committed to farming and producing organic wines. The magic happens in the vineyard with dry-farming, cover crops and more while the winemaking takes place in… the basement of Shaunt’s parents’ house in Orinda just east of Berkeley. The duo has used this space for over a decade because it allows them to make wine without the interferences of other wineries’ yeasts or other inputs. Named after the Japanese philosophy of finding beauty in imperfection, Wabi-Sabi is a field blend that is juicy with fresh raspberry, cherry, and vibrant acidity. Serve chilled! Organic, Unfiltered/Unfined & Native Yeast.

Lottiamo Toscana Montalcino: Perched 1,800 feet above sea level in southern Siena, Montalcino is arguably Tuscany's most storied hilltown. Lottiamo captures that essence with bright cherry and dried cranberry, earthy notes of leather, dried herbs and tobacco and the kind of food-friendly acidity that makes you want to reach for a bowl of pasta or a plate of aged Pecorino. The little orange fox has a special meaning too - in honor of a local kiddo, our friends at Impero Wine Distributors will donate $3 for each bottle sold to fight pediatric cancer.

 
Wine we’re drinking this week:

Personally, I'm not a big Chardonnay drinker. I lean more into big reds, bubbles, and crispy-but-not-too-crispy whites. However, I love bees. SO if anything comes into this shop with a bee on it or honey in it......you can bet I will be taking it home. Honey orange wheat beer? I’m buying it. A honey Kolsch? Permanent home in my fridge. A new native yeast Chardonnay with honey bees on the design? You have my attention..... Naturally, I had to do my service to the bee lovers community and give her a try.

Thankfully, this wasn’t just a “cute label got me again” situation. The Fableist Chardonnay comes out of California’s Central Coast and is made with a pretty hands-off philosophy: sustainably farmed fruit, native yeasts, minimal intervention, and a whole lot of personality. The winery itself leans heavily into storytelling, every wine is inspired by a different fable. And honestly that tracks because I'm sure many of you have tried the Fableist Cab Sauv with the big cicada on the front and it's comforting to know that was thanks to a story and not just an interesting design choice. The bee artwork on this bottle is inspired by the ancient tale of the honeybee and Jupiter, which makes me love it even more because apparently I’m emotionally incapable of resisting whimsical wine branding. Moral of the story is basically, "Be careful what you wish for." Unless you're wishing for a new Chardonnay, in which case I would advise you to throw caution to the wind.

In the glass, this Chardonnay lands somewhere beautifully between rich and fresh. You get layers of pear, peach, citrus blossom, a little native yeast earthiness, and this soft creamy texture without going full “movie theater butter bomb” (no shade, I love popcorn and I do in fact add an excessive amount of butter that probably would cause a coronary if given to a small horse). There’s a brightness to it that keeps everything lively, with subtle notes that remind me of apple blossom, and of course a tiny drizzle of honey. It feels cozy and sunny at the same time. Food-wise, this thing would absolutely shine with roast chicken, buttery pasta, seafood, salty cheeses, etc. Even as someone who usually reaches for other styles first, this bottle reminded me why Chardonnay became so popular in the first place, especially when it's done well. If you're looking for balanced, textured, and ridiculously easy to enjoy, this one is all the buzz. #savethebees

This week’s writeup is brought to you by Aidan.

 

May Tasting Events are spring ready!

 

Thanks for your business! See you soon, Beth, Julie & Aidan and the entire Brew Crew
